data.title: Argan: Don't Be Fooled Into Buying This Dip
data.description:
Don't Underestimate The Effects Of The Tariffs Mayhem

What a mayhem! President Trump, the so-called "tariff man" has caused consternation all over the market, as his on-again, off-again policy whiplash has engengered record-setting uncertainties in the market, as the bond markets even showed glimpses of spiraling out of control, although the fears of foreign investors dumping long-term US treasuries are likely overstated. Despite that, Trump’s “Blink Wednesday” also purveys investors an acute reminder about the plausibility of sudden policy u-turns, as the White House executes the president’s Art of the Deal with America’s trading partners, and of course China.

Like many companies involved in the industrials sector, Argan, Inc. (NYSE:AGX) has aptly warned investors about the need to prepare for tariffs and possible retaliations from trading partners as some of the most significant risks entailed in its business operations, as Argan elucidated in its 10K annual report filed at the end of March, a few days ahead of Trump’s Liberation Day on April 3. And we already know that businesses have ramped up their inventory in anticipation of these risks, while consumers have also bolstered their last minute spending, so I believe in the near term, the supply chain effects should be somewhat tempered for AGX.

As I highlighted in my previous Argan update, the company mainly drives its bullish thesis through the anticipated multi-year expansion and surge in electricity demand across AI data centers, manufacturing reshoring opportunities, and also the low-carbon/renewable energy & EV prospects. For the most part of January, I believe the deregulatory and tax cut, and pro-business agenda dominated the business and investing discourse, as the market was primed for a continuation of the optimism that preceded President Trump’s inauguration for his second term at the White House.

However, many business leaders were rightly stunned, as the US government applied shock therapy to compel a comprehensive economic restructuring that could upend "US exceptionalism," while also spurring a global trade slowdown. While we await for more good news from the White House with reference to its concurrent negotiations with trading partners on their respective reciprocal tariffs deals, the China risk could still set us back further as they have moved beyond the pure numbers game (tariff rates) into non-tariff barriers to target the US strategically. Consequently, supply chain resilience for the US economy could be put to the limit, given the convoluted global supply chain that American businesses like Argan are surely exposed to.

AGX has already alerted investors that the impact on [the then proposed] tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China could have an anticipated impact on “the cost of essential construction materials, such as steel and aluminium.” AGX also added that these are “critical components in power plant construction,” suggesting investors shouldn’t downplay the effects of the tariffs and future reprisals on AGX’s operating performance. Already, we have started to experience the impact on America’s tariffs on its neighbors having a marked impact on the aluminium businesses, and we will probably see more of these reports percolating through the economy.

Argan: Strong Backlog. But, Must Still Convert

The silver lining is probably that AGX’s revenue base is primarily in the US, although we still need to assess the impact from the supply chain on its almost $1.4B backlog that it accumulated in FY2025. While it marks a spectacular surge from FY2024, it remains to be seen whether FY2026 might yield another record setting acceleration opportunity, or could we even see a sharp drop off in CapEx spending for the data centers, which is identified as one of the main drivers of growth for AGX and also power demand and electricity generation.

The good news is, natural gas plants are still in demand by AI infrastructure providers, aligned to AGX’s current favorable posture in capitalizing on the industry's resilience to near-term demand shocks, while continuing to build up its long-term backlog for low-carbon or renewable solutions. However, the uncertainties associated with the White House’s aversion and objections to renewable energy projects could stall the forward outlook for this long-term growth vertical for AGX, suggesting we might have to be patient to see this thesis pan out.

Yet, the threat of having two key drivers (data centers and low-carbon projects) encountering noticeable headwinds might not be adequate to mitigate the reshoring optimism that has likely underpinned AGX’s recent recovery from its April 2025 lows. In other words, the qualms into what extent can America’s reshoring drive achieve remains questionable.

Business leaders must be confident that the high tariffs are here to stay and not face a reversal if they decide to reshore to America, even if we consider that inputs, import substitution and supply chain rewiring are possible. Otherwise, these CEOs might not be incentivized to move their well-established manufacturing bases, and consider pausing or delaying their projects until after they gain more clarity.

Moreover, we might also start to see more lawsuits being filed to change President Trump’s constitutional authority to bypass congress in setting these punitive tariffs, as they are starting to gain bipartisan support, as the GOP faces the risks of losing seats in congress in the mid term if the White House somehow manages to send the economy into a recession.

In other words, I believe the risks of AGX’s growth having stalled from this year isn’t immaterial, although the company has already cautioned that repeating a record FY2025 or even its final fiscal quarter seems unrealistic. Yet, the company believes that the growth momentum might recover in the latter half of this year, although I believe the current state of policy ambiguities suggest such optimism might need to be tempered in subsequent quarters, while contemplating a sharp deceleration in growth rates.

Argan: Faces Steep Revenue Growth Deceleration Risks

Revised AGX earnings estimates are aligned with management’s sanguine outlook on a possible pivot in growth rates from the second half of the 2025 calendar year, although questions about whether there’s a need to moderate our exuberance must be contemplated.

With the robust outlook communicated, it’s reasonable to expect a solid trajectory on its multi-year projects roadmap, suggesting AGX’s positivity isn’t assessed as misplaced. However, the threat of facing unforeseen cost increases that could scupper its margins projects, and the increased uncertainties linked to unexpected delays in its revenue conversion might affect growth inflection through the first half of 2026, if a recessionary outlook or a stagflationary scenario play out.

AGX: Valuation Too Pricey

That might have been more palatable, if AGX’s earnings multiples aren’t so aggressively configured, as the near-term cyclicality in its growth verticals might behoove steep multiples compressions if the growth outlook fails to pan out. Such are the high stakes for the company, and it's something that we can no longer afford to ignore under the current administration.

However, AGX’s forward EBITDA multiple of 13.9x remains at the two standard deviations zone over its 10Y average. Sure, bullish investors might argue that Argan is in a new advancement phase underpinned by secular growth drivers, and thus deserving of a higher valuation multiple.

Yet, the need to substantiate its valuations with what seems like pretty aggressive growth rates (in the current uncertain environment) seems too risky to contemplate. Moreover, when I gleaned AGX’s forward PEG ratio of 2.58, it’s almost 70% over its sector median, and analysts have continued to write down sector earnings estimates as I pen this update.

Can Argan really go against the falling tide for that much longer if economic repercussions and headwinds were to batter the US and global economy as the Trump tariffs turmoil rear its ugly head again? Probably, but I don’t think the current multiples give sufficient margin of safety for investors at all (if any).

Buy Or Sell AGX Stock?

When I parsed AGX’s long-term price chart, I believe it’s clear for all to see that the stock had a meteoric rise that arguably started in early 2024, while the market anticipated its massive backlog.

That panned out with the record $1.4B backlog it recorded, although we must always remember that the company has also cautioned that “project backlog amounts may be uncertain indicators of future revenues as project realization may be subject to unexpected adjustments, delays and cancellations.” Hence, we cannot simply assume definitive revenue conversion, and must scrutinize the multitudinous headwinds that could afflict Argan’s supply chain and infra spending in its most important verticals.

Moreover, AGX encountered stiff resistance which I also assessed to be a bull trap that rejected further advances above the $190 level, incidentally the stock’s January 2025 highs. The stock then cratered, falling more than 45% through its March lows, engulfing investors who bought near that January zenoth. However, it seems to have formed a bottom above the $100 level, which also coincides with the 61.8% fibonacci retracement level, auguring well for a potentially sustained bottom.

While I would normally have turned more optimistic after such deep pullbacks to shake out FOMO, I believe the bullish thesis could come under greater threats from the policy vissicitudes of the Trump administration, as we draw closer to the 90-day expiry for his tariff negotiations. Or the non-tariff barriers between the US and China might intensity supply chain risks, possibly hobbling trade flows into a trickle, even if they may not stop completely.

With the bull trap close to the $190 level possibly stemming further upside for now, while navigating what seems like a highly elevated valuation multiple, I urge investors not to buy this dip and recovery, but instead watch from the sidelines to see how it unfolds.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Netflix Saves Capitalism From Itself
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice and nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

Not Doomed Yet

As everyone knows, the market is doomed and the economy is dead. Liberalized capitalism is finished and we shall all soon be eking out a living in the poor house shoveling biomass into the furnace driving the nearest datacenter. Despite this existential threat, it seems folks are still ponying up for their Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) subscriptions. The company printed a solid quarter in Q1, and guided for another sound level of growth in Q2.

Now this tells us something about the market’s current travails. A leading consumer business is telling us that consumer spending is fine and is expected to continue to be fine. Earlier today, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) told the world that demand for advanced semiconductor devices was strong in Q1 and guided for more strength in Q2. Another breadcrumb telling us that the real economy is holding up better than the narrative-machines would have you believe.

At present, whilst there are some companies and some individuals already adversely affected by the tariff situation, the market selloff has happened in anticipation of more pain to come in the future, not in response to widespread real-economy problems stacking up. As always the market doesn’t know for sure whether more pain will come (and if so, if it will come from tariffs) or whether the Advil will kick in. Investors have sold first and will ask questions later.

Should trade agreements actually fall into place at any level the market deems acceptable, I would expect this market to resolve to the upside because as yet, the real economy is looking fine, so without the worry of what-if, we should see equity indices rise across the board.

Let’s take a look at NFLX numbers to evidence this argument.

Financial Fundamentals

Growth dropped a touch this quarter, to +13% YoY, but this remains very strong. Cash flow margins ticked up from 24% to 25% on a TTM unlevered pretax free cash flow basis. Net leverage ticked up slightly to 0.7x TTM UFCF but this remains very modest debt loading indeed. A very solid quarter.

Guidance? For growth to accelerate. That’s what TSMC said too. Acceleration into Q2. And unlike TSMC, NFLX demand won’t have suffered from any pull-forward effects due to tariffs. If NFLX management believe customer spending is going to see accelerating growth into the next quarter, it suggests the consumer is currently untouched by the rising risks of trade interruptions and costs.

So in the market today we seem to have a selloff caused by multiple compression in anticipation of an adverse hit to earnings arising from the current trade policy travails. Lack of resolution only amplifies these worries of course. And that suggests that if trade relations are settled by agreed tariff rates and other non-tariff matters, the market multiple of earnings can rise.

Valuation Analysis

NFLX today trades at some 11x TTM revenue / 43x TTM unlevered pretax FCF.

That's not cheap for 15% growth in revenues and in cash flows alike, but it isn’t prohibitive and doesn’t suggest to us that the stock should be sold due to excessive valuation.

Stock Chart

We think the stock can climb a little towards a 1.618 Wave 3 extension at $1165 before a selloff, a retest of the 2022 highs, then a final move up. We rate this name at Hold as a result. (Note that unlike most index leaders right now, the stock is holding over both its 50-day and 200-day moving averages).

Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 17 April 2025.

data.title: PLTY For Income Investors, Taming Palantir's Wild Ride
data.description:
I have covered a few YieldMax Income ETFs in my previous writings, and there is no single theme fits all take. The fundamental theory around writing cash secured calls and call spreads, and their pitfalls, is already in Yieldmax’s prospectus and well discussed.

I will evaluate the YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:PLTY) for what it aims to do despite the pitfalls, and that is generating stable sizeable income, riding the volatility of the underlying, while adequately preserving capital. And this I will do a little differently this time. For those who have been following me in my other analyses, will probably know that I usually look at the performance tenure (experience across market conditions), stability of returns and a total return analysis. Fundamentally, I am going to do the same, but in a slightly different way for PLTY, to arrive at a case for investing in it. I will take a set of most frequent scenarios and illustrate outcomes – where possible with data or else conceptually.

Scenario 1 : Underlying Crashes

PLTY was launched in October 2024 and does not have a lot of data or history to feel comfortable about its performance through market cycles – and income plans structured the way YieldMax plays, certainly depend a lot on market cycles when it comes to performance. This is something I have been trying to convey to my readers, who look at historical performance alone.

Feb 18, 2025 to Mar 10, 2025. Palantir (PLTR) crashed by about 38%. How did PLTY do? Did very well actually. Mirrored the fall in terms of the capital NAV. Overall PLTY fell by 40%, only marginally higher, but followed PLTR’s trail without additional losses.

Dividend payouts at the end of Feb 2025, saw a decent uptick, which is expected – almost double of previous distributions. So far PLTY follows the plot.

Scenario 2 : Underlying Shoots Up

Jan 14th 2025 to Feb 18th 2025. In a short span of a month, PLTR shot up 82%. This is a real test for PLTY. I am expecting either payouts to suffer or the underlying NAV capital in PLTY to take the hit as upside is capped. Let us check.

Sure enough, PLTY tracks the trend, but lags overall. At a time when PLTR has shot up 82% in quick time, the PLTY strategy has been able to capture only 55% uptrend. In conjunction with the scenario of sudden downturn discussed in Scenario 1, what this means is the capital went up by 55% and came down by 40% (both scenarios 1 and 2 combined), while the underlying went up by 82% and came down by 38%. Net impact after the two sided move, is that PLTR is now 15% up in all, while PLTY NAV is down by 9%.

The only payout during the upmove period was on Jan 30th 2025 at $2.98 (~2-5% of the NAV of PLTY during the period). And that does not make up for the opportunity loss overall. Taking both the payouts (during scenario 1 and scenario 2) during the uptrend and ensuring correction (the second payout as discussed earlier in scenario 1 being ~10% of PLTY NAV), the overall payouts do not mitigate the opportunity loss in the fund’s capital in a total return sense.

And that is exactly the point I have been trying to get across to my readers in the previous articles as well. Certain market conditions are not great for PLTY type income ETFs (while others are). Your expectation of the market condition is likely a critical factor to consider before investing. For very long term investors, you may end up matching performances at best, with a slightly lesser bumpy ride (in the best of cases). If the strategy underperforms, you will lose out in total return terms (in anything below an optimal performance case for PLTY).

Scenario 3 : Underlying Makes Volatile Moves

The juxtaposition of cases from Jan 15th to Mar 10th 2025 (scenario 1 and scenario 2), discussed as two separate scenarios earlier, replicates just that – a volatile market condition. We have seen the risk of overall underperformance for PLTY in this case. However, combining the payouts to the capital erosion, the actual performance mandate (despite underperformance) will depend on the investor's use case and risk appetite.

Certainly what we saw so far, cannot be called abysmal performance (underperformance in total return sense, yes, but not abysmally) in the conditions gauged. And then there will be other market conditions, where the performance will exceed the underlying’s and on average eventually provide a path of average capital preservation at decent returns, capping downside risks. Sounds good and PLTY follows that thesis thus far.

Scenario 4 : Underlying Goes Down Over Time

No such actual data in the short period of PLTY’s existence. But hypothetically, this is where PLTY should perform very well. Income from written calls should cushion the fall in underlying and return net income to investors in an era when PLTR is declining. PLTY should ace this market – provided the decline is smooth and gradual.

Scenario 5 : Underlying Goes Up Over Time

This is another market which serves PLTY well. In fact, in this case, depending on the structural setup of the options, the fund may gain as much as the underlying and add some income to the investor’s pocket from written calls expiring worthless.

Scenario 6: Underlying is Rangebound

Superimpose Scenario 3 and 4 for this. Again a strong case for an instrument like PLTY. No actual data exists to judge PLTY’s performance in such a situation– I go by what should be in theory.

Is PLTR the Right Underlying

Volatility is what I look for. Not too much such as MSTR, and not too low either. PLTR passes the lower threshold. We have seen the wild price swings in the last quarter alone. Average 252 day IV for PLTR is at 66. On the higher side, but well below MSTR’s 252 day average IV of 105. PLTR definitely qualifies as a good underlying to be able to generate decent income.

Premium Discount and Time of Entry

Strategies like PLTY will be generally need to be timed well for short to medium term investments. On that count, entering now fits the bill perfectly as IV remains elevated, but prices are stabilizing – implying a period of decent capital preservation at high payouts. Of course, it depends on your outlook for the markets going ahead. You need to be first bullish about PLTR and ideally time an entry just after a volatile period, like now. More volatility pending? That’s the call short term investments need to take.

Longer term investors have no such predicament. Over a very long term, markets will evenly dish out different cycles and the plan will go through periods of capital erosion, higher payouts and capital buildup, lower payouts, providing a smoother path to follow PLTR. Only beware – distributions cannot be sustained highs of the current projected 117% pa, understand that.

Another thing I usually check is the premium or discount to NAV, the fund trades at. In the case of PLTY, this is significantly high – deviations of up to 2% are noted. Clearly, that adds another timing dimension to fresh investments – usually I advise spreading the entry over days to minimize this impact, if transaction costs allow.

Final Call

As always, investing in PLTY should come with an indirect belief in PLTR (not in scope of this analysis). If you do and want to ride PLTR with reduced volatility and decent payouts, I would recommend a Buy for PLTY. Timing is right just about now for medium term investors. Long term investors can enter anytime, better now.

Expectations of payout yield should be moderated, but PLTY should most likely provide investors two things – 1) an easier ride on PLTR than owning the stock directly and 2) beat most other kinds of income plans in the longer term (but not at 117% pa return levels).

data.title: IBM Acquiring Hakkoda Enhances Its Transformation From It's Legacy Businesses
data.description:
International Business Machines (NYSE:IBM) has made quite a comeback, as they were written off as a legacy technology company that wasn't adapting to the emerging landscape when Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) exited its position. Over the past 5-years, shares of IBM have made new all-time highs as they spun off their legacy infrastructure businesses to focus on larger ROI opportunities, including cloud, consulting, and artificial intelligence. The strategy has worked, and I remain bullish on IBM, especially after IBM announced that it had acquired Hakkoda to expand its consulting data transformation services. There are still some skeptics regarding how impactful artificial intelligence will be, but the largest companies are committed to allocating hundreds of billions toward CapEx to further their artificial intelligence offerings. IBM has entrenched itself in the technologies of tomorrow, and EPS growth is still projected on the horizon. As the technological landscape changes, I believe that IBM has gotten itself in a position to reap the rewards of where future capital is deployed. I think the future still looks bright, and IBM can be an investment that will thrive in the future.

Following up on my previous article about IBM

I wrote an article about IBM in the middle of January (can be read here), and since then, shares have increased by 10.70% while the S&P 500 has declined by -7.89%. When the dividend is factored in IBMs, the total return is 11.44% over this period. IBM has continued to be a solid investment for investors as it has led to large amounts of capital appreciation with modest dividend income. In my previous article, I discussed how I felt IBM's valuation was still attractive after a strong Q3 report in addition to the opportunities I felt they could capitalize on in the future. I am following up with a new article to discuss how the Hakkoda acquisition can further IBM's transformation and position them for further growth in the future. IBM has proved many bears wrong over the past several years, and I think there is a lot more upside potential and dividend income for long-term investors.

Risks to investing in IBM

While I am still bullish on IBM after making new all-time highs, there are risks to consider before allocating capital to them. Despite IBM being a well-established company focusing on high-growth segments, including cloud computing and artificial intelligence, IBM operates in a competitive industry that is under pressure from the largest companies in the market. IBM's cloud infrastructure represents 2% of the total market as they are significantly behind Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOGL). The large lead from the AMZN, MSFT, and GOOGL could negatively impact IBM's ability to expand its market share in cloud computing. While IBM has pivoted and spun off its legacy business, it still relies on its consulting and infrastructure segments to generate a significant portion of its revenue. If the consulting business slows down or its infrastructure business doesn't innovate quickly compared to its competitors, the market's perception of it could change and take the valuation lower. IBM also has $49 billion in long-term debt, which costs them $1.7 billion in interest expenses. If the Fed takes rates higher, then IBM's margins could compress and impact profitability. Investors should conduct their own due diligence as we could have seen the top in IBM's share price in Q1 2025.

The Hakkoda deal is a strategic acquisition for IBM that will further its entrenchment into artificial intelligence consulting

After acquiring HashiCorp to help build its cloud platform, IBM landed another deal to strengthen its consulting business by acquiring Hakkoda. Hakkoda specializes in data transformation and AI-driven solutions through its consulting business. Hakkoda is an award-winning partner of Snowflake (SNOW), which has established its reputation for migrating, modernizing, and monetizing data throughout the enterprise data management sector. Hakkoda has a portfolio of generative AI-powered assets that accelerate its engagements and strengthen IBM's client experiences. This acquisition aligns with IBM's strategic goals as IBM has heavily invested in artificial intelligence and hybrid cloud solutions since shedding its legacy business. Hakkoda's capabilities in data platform management and artificial intelligence expand and strengthen IBM's consulting business by offering a more comprehensive solution to its clients looking to optimize their data and monetize their digital assets. This acquisition advances IBM's commitment to being a leading consulting firm to help enterprise-scale clients compete in the age of artificial intelligence and gain a competitive advantage over their competitors that aren't making these strategic investments.

I am very bullish on this move by IBM, as Hakkoda's advantage is its ability to migrate, modernize, and help clients monetize their data, with the help of their alignment with SNOW. Hakkoda's expertise in these emerging technologies is critical as enterprises are expanding their appetite to build integrated data platforms that are optimized for speed, cost, and efficiency through leveraging AI-driven operations. Hakkoda's focus on industry-specific solutions, including healthcare & life sciences, and financial services, insurance, supply chain & logistics, retail & CPG, and public sector will not only bring in more customers to IBM's portfolio but expand IBM's ability to service its existing clients in these areas. IBM will be able to offer advanced artificial intelligence solutions and enhance its client's competitive edge. Once Hakkoda's generative AI tools and assets are integrated into IBM's platform, IBM's consulting team will have the ability to deliver results faster and more efficiently for its clients. I believe that the combination of assets will be a key differentiator in the market and make IBM's consulting services more attractive as companies leverage the use of artificial intelligence.

IBM's consulting business is its 2nd largest business segment, as it generated $20.7 billion in revenue during the 2024 fiscal year. While the consulting business only grew by 1% in 2024, it achieved high single-digit growth over a 3-year period, which aligns with IBM's previous projections. IBM had a record-breaking Q4 in signings growth with 23%, which is the highest in its recent history, while producing an 8% YoY increase in its backlog, which indicates strong demand for its consulting services. IBM's GenAI business has grown to over $5 billion since its inception, of which 80% comes from consulting services. I believe that the Hakkoda deal will help expand IBM's consulting revenue, and it could provide its software business with a multiplier effect as the consulting team cross-sells its clients with IBM's software products.

IBM still looks undervalued, and I think there is an opportunity for shares to go higher

When I look through IBM's financials, I see strong margins and increased levels of profitability. Over the past 3-years, IBM has increased its annual revenue by $5.57 billion (9.67%). IBM's gross profit has grown by $4.07 billion (12.91%) over this period to $35.55 billion. This is a gross profit margin of 57.3%, which has increased from 55.66% in 2021. IBM finished 2024 generating $12.4 billion in free cash flow (FCF) which was an increase of 15.49% since 2021. IBM is operating at a 19.98% FCF margin, which is very enticing considering its FCF growth rate. On the bottom line, IBM generated just over $6 billion in net income in 2024, which was a 4.88% increase since 2021. IBM has a 9.71% profit margin and continues to utilize its profitability to make acquisitions and return capital to its shareholders. In 2024, IBM paid $6.15 billion to its investors in dividends while repurchasing $651 million worth of shares.

In addition to becoming a company that has reinvented itself and growing its top and bottom line, IBM has also become a Dividend Aristocrat. IBM has grown its dividends over the past 25 years, and over the past 5 years, the dividends have grown by 1.54%. IBM is paying a dividend of $6.68, which is a 2.8% yield. The payout ratio on IBM's dividend is 64.57%, so there is a lot of room in their EPS to continue future increases. In an environment where the Fed will likely start cutting and work its way to a more neutral, effective rate, IBM's dividend will be growing, and investors can lock in a 2.8% yield at today's levels. When I look back at the first article I wrote about IBM in June 2018 (can be read here), IBM has appreciated by 72.93%, and its total return is 146.71% as more than double the return has been provided through its dividend. IBM was drastically undervalued back then, but these metrics show how powerful a strong dividend program can be in boosting a company's total return.

The analyst community is expecting IBM to continue its EPS expansion over the next several years. IBM is projected to generate $10.77 of EPS in 2025, and it currently trades at 20.88 times 2025 earnings. Over the next 2 years, IBM is expected to grow its EPS by 14.3% from $10.77 to $12.31. Investors are still paying 19.38 times 2027 earnings for IBM, which is still a bargain despite IBM trading for above $200 per share. IBM is also expected to grow its revenue on a YoY basis at the mid-single-digit percentage rate, and I think that the latest acquisition of Hakkoda could add additional EPS to the bottom line. IBM is trading at an attractive valuation, especially since enterprises are still in their infancy of deploying their artificial intelligence strategy. The next several years are setting up well for IBM, and I think that we will see several upward revisions in earnings along the way.

Conclusion

IBM's transformation from a legacy tech giant to a leader in cloud computing, consulting, and artificial intelligence showcases its ability to adapt and deliver on its strategic vision. The recent acquisition of Hakkoda has sent a message that IBM will continue building out its enterprise offerings in these areas as it doubles down on the commitment to its strategic vision. This move bolsters IBM's consulting arm with specialized expertise in data transformation and AI-driven solutions, positioning it to meet the rising enterprise demand for innovative technologies. Despite risks such as fierce competition from larger companies and a reliance on its consulting and infrastructure segments, IBM is generating $12.4 billion in free cash flow, which allows it to continue paying a large dividend and make acquisitions. I believe IBM is trading at an attractive valuation with projected EPS growth of 14.3% over the next two years. IBM has produced a compelling blend of capital appreciation and steady income over the past several years, and I believe its recent string of acquisitions will allow IBM to continue this trend. As enterprise clients continue to allocate capital toward their artificial intelligence and cloud infrastructure, IBM is in a strong position to continue its forward growth and generate returns for shareholders.

data.title: CSX Corporation: Treasuries Look Better
data.description:
It’s been about 3 months since I wrote an article titled “CSX: Continue to Avoid. (NASDAQ:CSX). You’ll never guess what my recommendation was. Since then, the shares are down about 16% against a loss of about 13% for the S&P 500, so my very fragile, underdeveloped ego feels somewhat vindicated. I wish this article could be one 1,200 word victory lap, full of self congratulation, but unfortunately you people wouldn’t find that all that interesting, so I’ve got to write about the stock. The company has just announced again, which is what put the name back on my radar. I want to work out whether or not it makes sense to buy at current levels. After all, a stock trading at $27.50 is a much less risky investment than when it’s trading at $33. I’ll make the determination by reviewing the financials, and by comparing the stock to the risk free investment.

As I say repeatedly, and no doubt tiresomely, if a stock isn’t likely to offer superior returns to the risk free alternative, why would you buy it? Stocks come with risk. Treasuries come with risks too, but are far fewer. Therefore the former should offer you superior returns. I hope this point isn’t controversial.

Welcome to my thesis statement. This is the part of the article where I give you the summation of my thoughts pretty early so you have the option to go off and do something more productive with your time than exposing yourself to my self congratulatory screed, questionable humour, and proper spelling. You’re welcome. I’m of the view that CSX’s financial performance was uninspired, and I think this continues a trend that’s been in place for a few years now. Additionally, the dividend would need to grow by an unrealistic amount for stock holders to receive the same cash as Treasury Note holders. If you’re of the view that capital gains may make up for the shortfall to the risk free rate, tell that to people who bought exactly 1 year ago, and are down by about 19%. So, I’m going to continue to recommend avoiding this name until the yield spikes, and/or earnings improve nicely. Thus ends my thesis statement. If you read on from here, that’s on you. I don’t want to read any moaning in the comments section about my awful humour or proper pronunciation of the word “oot.”

Financial Snapshot

In my view, the latest financial results are unimpressive. Compared to the same period last year, revenue and net income are down by 7%, and 26.6% respectively. Operating income is lower by about 22%. If we zoom out a bit, we see that operating income in the quarter just ended is lower by about 14.6% than the first quarter of 2019. I think a reasonable argument could be made to suggest that the railroad has entered a period of slow growth. My evidence for this claim is that revenue in 2024 was lower than it was in 2023, and 2023 was lower than the previous year. So, I don’t expect much in the way of growth for the foreseeable future. This is why the payot ratio has climbed from ~21% to 38.25% today.

Things don’t look much better when we consider the state of the capital structure. Long term debt is higher by about $625 million, or 3.4%, and cash is down by about $336 million, or 23%. Zooming out once again, long term debt is higher today than it was in 2019 by about $3.36 billion, or 21%.

Although the financial performance was unimpressive, I think the dividend is reasonably secure. For that reason, I’d be willing to buy the shares if they’re reasonably priced.

The Stock v Treasury Note

I want to make two points at this juncture, so I’m going to make two points at this juncture. First, in the world of investing, everything’s relative. Second, we’re always looking for the greatest risk-adjusted returns. Please take note of that last sentence my fellow stock investors. We're not seeking "returns." We're seeking "risk-adjusted returns." One of the implications of this point is that a stock, which is generally riskier than the 10 Year Treasury Note, must offer a better return than debt backed by the full faith and credit of Uncle Sam. Put another way, if a stock can’t generate the same level of cash flow as the risk-free rate, why on Earth would you buy it?

In terms of the specifics of comparing a given stock to the Treasury Note, I get at the question “at what rate will the dividend need to grow over the next decade for stock investors to receive the same cash flows as Treasury Note holders?” Please note that I think they should receive more than Treasury holders, given the risks involved, but I’ll burn that bridge when I get to it. So at the moment, the yield on the 10 Year Treasury Note is about 4.4%, and the dividend yield on CSX is about 1.9% at the moment. Per the table below, the dividend would need to grow at a CAGR of about 17.7% for the shareholder to receive the same cash flows as the Treasury Note holder over the next decade. In my view, that’s a very high hurdle, and I think the probability that that growth rate will be achieved is about 0.

A bull will probably argue at this point about the “unassailable moat”, and the fact that dividends aren’t the only source of returns for stockholders. Depending on the stock, capital appreciation also plays a huge part in the returns earned by shareholders. That’s true, both stocks and Treasury Notes hold out the promise of earning a capital gain. I’d say two things in response. First, that argument was just as valid a year ago, and the shares are down a little over 14% since then, so what goes up may also come down. This is where the excess risk in stock investing comes into play. So, you may make more on capital gains, but you “pay” for those returns by taking on more risk, so it’s a wash in my view. Second, Treasury Notes can also generate significant capital gains. I’m of the view that bond yields are, over the long term, a function of growth and inflation expectations, I’m not sanguine about either of those, so I’m of the view that rates are likely heading lower at some point. This drop in rates may offer a decent lift in the price of Treasuries. Being long winded is “on brand” for me, and what I just wrote is a pretty verbose passage to demonstrate that you can earn a capital gain in Treasuries too. The advantage of holding the Treasury is that you make a pretty decent, fully guaranteed cash return while you wait.

Given the above, my feelings haven’t really changed. Treasuries remain relatively attractive, so I’m going to continue to recommend avoiding this name. I’ll become much more interested if the stock starts to trade in the low $20s.

data.title: Gray Media: A Value Play On A Highly Anticipated 2026 Midterm Election Season
data.description:
It's said that the stock market is forward-looking by around six months. If that is the case, value investors who also happen to have fairly high-risk tolerance should put Gray Media, Inc. (NYSE:GTN) on their radar right now. Gray Media is the largest owner of top-rated local television stations and digital assets in the United States. It serves 113 television markets that collectively reach approximately 37% of US television households. As one would expect with a rapidly changing media landscape, GTN's legacy television business has been on a slow, downward decline. As has its stock price since 2022. However, there was a brief period of time from October 2023 to January 2024 when the stock rose by more than 50%. As the United States was heading into a highly anticipated election season, political spending on advertising was top of mind. That instinct ended up being correct as GTN achieved a $3.36 EPS last year on the strength of its political ad revenue. An absurd EPS figure relative to today's stock price of around the same number.

GTN falling to this level is not completely without merit. Its expected weak 2025 performance and large debt burden are legitimate concerns. However, I believe that GTN management just needs to hold the fort during this trough, as it won't be too much longer before the 2026 midterm election season is upon us. Without getting too political, there are factions of people in the United States who are already looking forward to the midterm elections. I believe that we will see record levels of interest and engagement from the voting population for a midterm election. That high level of interest will result in a lot of political ad spending. Spending that will run straight to GTN's bottom line.

Reviewing GTN's Q4 2024 earnings release, we see a year-over-year 11% increase in revenue for the full year and a turn from a $1.39 per share loss in 2023 to a $3.36 EPS in 2024. This variance is all driven by political advertising, which was $497 million in 2024 compared to $79 million in 2023. Simply excluding political advertising is not a good way to compare year-over-year results because the jump in political advertising will cannibalize non-political ad revenue. The best way to compare growth is to look at 2022 to 2024 when political ad revenue is high for both years. The company is well aware of this and provides that data within its release:

A combination of essentially flat revenue and increasing costs from 2022 to 2024 shows that the business is challenged. Combine that with the fact that a good chunk of the $3.36 EPS seen in 2024 will be given back in losses in 2025 and the P/E ratio of around 1x makes more sense. Q1 2025 revenue guidance has a midpoint of $770 million, down 6.5% from Q1 2024 revenue of $823 million and down 3.9% from Q1 2023 revenue of $801 million.

GTN has a high debt level, and servicing it is cutting into its profitability. Interest expense was $485 million, $440 million, and $354 million for 2024, 2023, and 2022, respectively. Given flat-lining revenue, that trend cannot continue. The company has made headway in reducing its debt load. Long-term debt decreased from $6.1 billion to $5.6 million from the end of 2023 to the end of 2024. An additional $250 million in debt has been authorized to be bought back on the open market this year. The reduction in debt has led the company to project $450 million in interest expense for 2025.

From an operating standpoint, there is more bad than good, but the market may be punishing GTN a bit too much for it. GTN currently ranks as an A+ on Seeking Alpha's valuation grade. Though that grade might be a little overstated to reality.

GTN's GAAP P/E of 1x is obviously far below the sector median. However, due to the nature of one good year, one-off year, the forward P/E has no meaning and therefore no grade. Due to the high debt load compared to its market cap, EV/EBITDA multiples are a better measure of GTN's relative valuation. In GTN's "good" year, its multiple is about half of the sector median. In the "bad" year, it's about 15% too high. That would suggest to me that the stock price is undervalued by about 25%. GTN trades well below book value, but considering that a high proportion of its assets are intangibles or illiquid with a heavy debt load, I don't consider its discount to book to be a driving force of value. The final clincher in favor of GTN for me is its low Price to Cash Flow metric. Despite the business being stagnant and persistent challenges in navigating shifts in the way the public consumes media, the company generates cash today. Whether it's a good year or a bad year. The operating cash flow for the last three years was $751 million in 2024, $648 million in 2023 and $829 million in 2022. Enough to pay down debt. Enough to pay the quarterly $0.08 dividend, which currently yields nearly 10% at today's stock price.

Conclusion: On my watch list for a potential buy

Some companies are considered good businesses but with an expensive stock. Gray Media is the mirror opposite. The business isn't great, but it's very cheap. I'll give credit to management; it's doing what it needs to do to survive the down years while putting up big numbers during election years. Right now, the stock has no momentum, and election ad season might be a few months too far out even for a forward-looking market. So, there is no point in rushing in just yet. However, I think GTN's revenue potential as well as the hype around its revenue potential once election season nears will be larger than it has ever been. Time it just right and there is potential for a profitable investment in a short timeframe. Therefore, I am giving GTN a buy rating while adding it to my watchlist in preparation for a possible purchase in a few weeks or months.

data.title: Retire With A Stress-Free 9%-Yielding Monthly Paying Dividend Portfolio
data.description:
Retiring on dividends requires that you have not only a sufficient amount of current income to cover your living expenses, but also you need that income to be sustainable over the long term, while also growing at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time. You can take two approaches to growing your income over time: you can either generate it through dividend per share growth in your underlying holdings, or you can generate it through retaining some of your dividend income and reinvesting it to buy additional shares of dividend-paying stocks.

Of course, if you take the latter approach, you need to make sure that your dividend income comfortably exceeds your living expenses so that you can be sure to have extra capital on hand every month to buy additional shares. You need to make sure that you have the self-discipline necessary to not spend that money because if you do not, while you may not notice it in the short term, over the long term, prices are going to start rising, and your passive income is not going to rise along with it.

With that principle in view, in this article I am going to share how you can build a pretty simple yet well-diversified and sustainable stream of passive income that yields 9% on a weighted average basis, giving you as an investor plenty of disposable income to both meet your living expenses as well as grow your passive income to keep up with inflation over the long term. To do this, you can simply own three funds that will give you broad diversification across many sectors with a particularly heavy weighting in real asset-backed, recession-resistant sectors that can help your income hold up well during even fairly sharp economic downturns.

Portfolio Component #1

The first fund is the most diversified. It is the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). Effectively, it is an S&P 500 Index (SPY) fund that implements a notional covered call strategy that targets a 1% monthly yield from the options premiums generated. Additionally, by using Section 1256 contracts, SPYI enjoys tax benefits with about 60% of the options premium distributions being taxable at long-term rates and 40% at short-term rates, which is very favorable compared to the typical treatment of options premium income, which is typically all treated as short-term capital gains or ordinary income.

SPYI is great in that it offers investors broadly diversified exposure to some of the world's largest and highest quality companies, including the Magnificent Seven, while still paying out a very attractive income stream by creating a more stable sequence of returns through selling covered calls. On the flip side, as I pointed out in a recent article, this strategy does have the downside of somewhat locking in permanent losses whenever there is a sharp market crash followed by a V-shaped recovery, as the covered calls keep the underlying holdings from bouncing back sharply from losses in their entirety. As a result, it is important to not make this the core part of your portfolio, and so in this article, I would only make it a 33% weighting and have the other two holdings also at 33% weighting.

Portfolio Component #2

The next fund that I would go to is the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG), which is a CEF that invests primarily in utilities stocks, as its name indicates. It also holds some broader infrastructure companies on top of that, giving it a bit of exposure into that segment, which does not occupy a large percentage at all of SPYI, further enhancing the diversification of the portfolio. Additionally, the defensive nature of utilities and infrastructure makes it a good holding for a durable passive income stream, as the regulated and contracted nature of utilities and infrastructure assets means that they tend to hold up very well during periods of economic weakness.

This is also evidenced in the fact that UTG has not only sustained its dividend over the long term but has actually grown it.

On top of that, UTG is fairly attractively priced right now as it currently trades at a discount to its net asset value, despite on average trading at a slight premium to its net asset value. It also has a very attractive 7.3% dividend yield, has relatively low leverage below 20%, which further reduces risks during a downturn, and it also has pretty low management fees compared to other actively managed CEFs, such as one of its peer competitors in the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

Another reason to like UTG is that it has outperformed its broader sector (XLU) over the long term, giving us confidence that the active management running the fund knows what they are doing, and not only generates attractive long-term total returns for shareholders but also sustains and grows the payout accordingly.

This should give retirees a good bit of confidence in the fund manager's ability to responsibly allocate capital and meet the fund's long-term objectives.

Portfolio Component #3

The third and final fund that can meet the goals of a portfolio like this is the InfraCap REIT Preferred ETF (PFFR). It is a pretty simple fund that is diversified across a broad range of REIT (VNQ) preferreds while paying out very attractive monthly dividends that currently come in at around an 8.2% annualized yield.

The beauty of this fund is that it also charges a fairly attractive 0.45% expense ratio and offers investors effectively preferred equity in a large cross-section of institutional, commercial, and residential real estate, which makes it quite a durable source of income. This is because real estate, by nature, tends to be a more stable source of massive income due to the highly contracted and essential nature of real estate. On top of that, a preferred position in the capital stack makes the income even safer as preferred dividends have to be paid out before common dividends are paid out, and the REIT status of these companies means that they have to pay out at least 90% of their taxable income as dividends in order to retain their income tax-exempt status.

Moreover, the real asset nature of REITs makes them more conservative because these companies can always sell off or mortgage individual pieces of real estate (or in the case of a mortgage REIT, their loans are backed by liquid hard assets) to get out of financially challenging situations much more easily than many other business models can. This means that in the case of REIT preferreds, even if the company enters into some operational and/or financial challenges and has to eliminate their common dividends, preferred dividends are cut only in rare circumstances. REITs also generally only very rarely go bankrupt, providing a long-term sense of safety and recoverability to these REIT preferreds. This is also true because even in events where REIT preferred dividends have to be reduced or suspended, the vast majority of them are cumulative in nature, which means that there is a good chance that those dividends will eventually be recovered by shareholders. Given that the fund is so broadly diversified as well across individual holdings, it even further reduces the risks associated with its investment portfolio, since even if one or two of those REITs do have such significant problems that their preferred dividends have to be reduced or eliminated altogether, it will only have a very minor impact on the overall yield of the fund.

When you combine these strengths with the fund's very attractive yield and the goal of this portfolio to generate sustainably high passive income, some of which will be used to meet living expenses and some of which will be used to be reinvested to keep up with the gross effects of inflation, PFFR seems like a really good fit. This is even more the case when you consider the fact that neither SPYI nor UTG have significant real estate exposure, so PFFR nestles in quite nicely into this portfolio, while further accentuating its recession resistance.

Investor Takeaway

When you combine a 33% weighting in SPYI with a 33% weighting in UTG and a 33% weighting in PFFR, you get a roughly 9% weighted average annualized dividend yield. Additionally, all three of these funds pay out their dividends on a monthly basis, so you effectively get a 0.75% yield paid out each month like clockwork. Combining that with the broadly diversified and fairly conservative posture of this portfolio makes it a remarkably simple yet powerful passive income stream, making it potentially a good fit for a retiree's portfolio, though, you should, of course, consult with a personal financial advisor before building your retirement income portfolio. This is the sort of approach that we take, albeit with even more favorable risk-reward, through mostly individual common stock, preferred stock, and baby bond selections in our retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Bank OZK: Solid Q1 And Poised To Generate Value Amidst Trade Uncertainty
data.description:
Bank OZK overview

Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) has been a mixed performer this year, with shares essentially flat from a year ago. That change masks a significant amount of volatility, as OZK has faced concerns about its commercial real estate (CRE) exposure, while its underlying results have actually been solid. Shares have not been immune to the market sell-off, but the stock jumped 6% on Thursday in response to strong quarterly results. I last covered shares in January, reiterating them as a “strong buy,” but the stock has lost 18% since then given economic fears. After Q1 results, I remain very bullish.

Balance sheet growth remains strong

In the company’s first quarter, Bank OZK earned $1.47, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue rose marginally to $409 million. Earnings were down 2.5% from last year, as the company has faced narrowing margins from the Fed’s rate cuts. OZK also continues to invest in growth, aiming to add 34 branches this year. This is driving a 10% increase in non-interest expense in 2025, but as these branches ramp, that should support ongoing deposit growth. With its strong capital position, OZK can take advantage of volatility to grow. Plus, it executed on $35 million of repurchases so far in April, given the share price drop.

OZK remains one of the fastest-growing regional banks I follow. Deposit rose by $900 million sequentially to $31.9 billion. This continues with its trajectory of growth over several years. Deposits are up about $2.5 billion from last year, more than 8%. We did see a 29bp decline in interest-bearing deposit costs in the quarter, given lower short-term interest rates. I expect to see ongoing deposit cost improvement. In Q2, $5.6 billion of deposits matured at 4.56% billion, and in Q3, $7.1 billion matured at 4.4%. It should be able to roll deposits below 4%, helping to reduce interest costs.

Given its deposit growth, OZK has been able to aggressively grow its loan book. It now has $31.1 billion of loans, up $1.1 billion sequentially. While Q1 was strong, OZK is maintaining guidance for mid-to-upper single-digit loan growth this year. Given the economic uncertainty, loan demand could slow, so I believe it prudent to retain guidance rather than assume this elevated pace of growth continues. It also has higher scheduled loan maturities in Q2-Q4, which will be a headwind for growth.

Interest margin pressures are manageable

Now, despite this loan growth, OZK’s $375 million of net interest income (NII) was down about 0.6%. That is because loan yields of 7.79% were down 38bps sequentially and 91bps from last year. Nearly 90% of its loan book is floating rate, and so these move down quickly in response to a lower Fed funds rate, while deposits take more time to roll over. Because of this, its net interest margin (NIM) of 4.31% was down 2bps sequentially and 40bps from last year. If Fed funds remain flat, NIM would rise, but further rate cuts would narrow NIM as loans reprice faster than deposits.

One cushion against a sharply lower fed funds rate is that the majority of loans have floors within 100bps of current levels, which will slow loan yield declines even as deposit yields continue to fall. Additionally, its $3 billion investment portfolio has a 4.03% yield, up from 2.93% a year ago. This was up 76bps sequentially, given the maturities of low-yielding bonds. Another $400 million matures this year at 3.17%, which likely creates positive reinvestment opportunities. Given a growing asset base and ongoing deposit cost cuts, I expect to see NII rise from Q1, even if NIM faces ongoing pressure.

OZK's credit quality remains strong

The bigger concern in the past for OZK has been loan losses given its CRE exposure. 62% of loans are from its real estate specialties group. This is down from a peak of 70%, but still, OZK has one of the higher exposures to the asset class. Its portfolio is well-balanced with multifamily its largest category. Across subsectors, its loan-to-value ratios are very conservative, which should greatly mitigate losses given default.

Of course, in real estate, having an accurate appraised value is critical. Another positive to me is that the vast majority of its exposure was issued in 2022 or later. Almost all of its exposure is post-COVID and much comes well after the Fed began its hiking cycle. These events were headwinds for many CRE asset-type valuations, but they were known when OZK underwrote its loans. It is not as though it is carrying office loans underwritten pre-COVID before hybrid work became a significant factor.

This is why I continue to feel its potential loan losses are manageable. Indeed, actual underwriting results remain strong. It had a modest 25bps of net-charge offs. This is a bit above last year’s 20bp level, but it still remains low. It added $19 million to reserves for $639 million, consistent with its gradual reserve build over the past two years. Reserves assume a greater combined risk of a downside or stagflation scenario than a baseline one. With $214 million of nonperforming assets, OZK has about 3x coverage, which I view as very strong, especially given its loan book is largely secured.

Interestingly, if tariffs lead to a corporate-led economic downturn, commercial real estate may not be the problematic sector for banks that it has been widely expected to be. Instead, banks with more C&I (or business) lending could face higher losses. Of course, commercial real estate will not be immune from slower economic activity, which would dampen lease demand. Still, CRE has less direct tariff exposure than businesses or consumers. If anything, tariffs likely increase replacement costs for buildings, which could slow construction activity. Lower supply may support existing properties.

Capital is excellent, and OZK shares are very attractive

I continue to believe OZK is well-positioned from a credit quality perspective. On top of this, it is very well capitalized. It has an 11.32% CET1 ratio and is not saddled with the large unrealized losses on an outsized securities portfolio like other banks. Its strong capital position enables it to take advantage of opportunities to grow when other banks have to pull back. This is also evident in its ability to step in and buy back stock after a large decline. It also increased its dividend for the 59th quarter and offers a 4.3% dividend yield.

Back in January, I saw at least $6 in EPS and viewed 9–10x earnings as fair given concerns about potential CRE losses. Assuming 2-3 rate cuts, I continue to view OZK as likely to earn about $6.00, assuming $20-30 million of quarterly reserve builds. One risk is that loan growth could be a bit slower if demand slows. I do not expect its net charge-offs to move meaningfully even with slower growth, given the nature of its loan book.

Shares are about 7x earnings, which I view as exceptionally attractive, and OZK is a proverbial “baby thrown out with the bathwater” during this market sell-off. Ongoing opportunistic repurchases may also help to support shares. Its strong capital position also leaves OZK better positioned than peers like Regions (RF) or Ally (ALLY) during a prolonged downturn, in my view. As such, I continue to view OZK as one of the most compelling opportunities in the market. Its strong reaction to Q1 results was justified, and I do expect shares to outperform, with a fair value still $55–60.

data.title: Traders Should Exit Hertz Following Recent Short Squeeze
data.description:
Is it 2020 all over again? We once again have global supply chain disruptions, worries about a potential recession, and traders looking to the government for deals and rate cuts to try to simulate the economy.

And, like in 2020, rental car company Hertz (NYSE:HTZ) appears to be in perilous financial condition. As of earlier this month, the company is fighting with bondholders, and it is hiring advisors to work through a potential capital restructuring program to deal with its more than $6 billion debt load.

In 2020, Hertz infamously filed bankruptcy and then saw its share price skyrocket as meme traders gravitated to Hertz stock for whatever reason. After an initial hundreds of percent rally following bankruptcy, however, reality set back in. Hertz stock traded back down and was ultimately delisted. The Hertz excitement, however, would be a precursor to the meme stock moves that were soon to come in companies such as GameStop (GME) and AMC Entertainment (AMC).

And now, the original meme stock has returned. Hertz shares relisted in 2021 after exiting bankruptcy. Shares traded down throughout the past couple years. But, with well-known investor Bill Ackman disclosing a large position in the company this week, HTZ stock suddenly doubled over the past two trading days:

This move isn't quite as shocking as the rally in 2020. In that prior situation, Hertz had already filed bankruptcy and then the momentum traders stepped in and bid the stock up stratospherically. Here, Hertz is in apparent financial distress, but there are still possible avenues to avoiding an eventual bankruptcy restructuring. In any event, it seems lightning has struck twice in the case of this debt-laden car rental firm finding sudden popularity with traders.

What Ackman Sees In Hertz

In a post on social media website X, formerly Twitter, Pershing Square manager Bill Ackman offered a lengthy rationale for the firm's position in Hertz shares.

Ackman explained that his fund had acquired a 19.8% stake in Hertz through a combination of stock and total return swaps. He describes a company which had lost its way due to ordering too many electric vehicles from Tesla (TSLA). These EVs did not resonate with consumers and also declined in value leading to losses upon resale. Ackman believes, however, that the worst is behind Hertz and that the company can rebound for the following reasons:

  • Better rental car industry profitability
  • Resolution of the Tesla fleet issues
  • Successful turnaround plan
  • Highly-leveraged balance sheet can lead to attractive shareholder returns

Ackman has some notes on industry structure which are interesting but arguably not all that relevant to the near-term investment case. Hertz has a huge pile of debt and needs sharply improved near-term results to move the needle. On that point, however, Ackman sees a big potential catalyst from the tariffs. He wrote that:

"Hertz owns a fleet of over 500,000 vehicles valued at approximately $12 billion. A 10% increase in used car prices would equate to a $1.2 billion gain on its auto assets – equivalent to approximately half of the company’s current market capitalization."

This sounds exciting at face value. However, $1.2 billion makes much less of an impact than it might seem at first glance. The company has more than $6 billion in debt in addition to a chunky stack of other liabilities. Gains on vehicle sales -- should they occur -- are likely to go toward paying down and refinancing debt. I don't believe potential gains against the firm's market capitalization is the most relevant of comparisons. Any gains on car sales are better understood in the context of the firm's far larger total enterprise value. Additionally, I expect that new car prices will rise, potentially considerably, due to the tariffs and largely offset the near-term benefits from higher used car prices through a full economic cycle.

Ackman offers an upside bullish case based on a 2029 valuation that supposes $2 billion of annualized adjusted EBITDA. Given Hertz's struggles with profitability for quite a few years now, I would take 2029 assumptions of robust profitability with a grain of salt. It could happen, but investors can be forgiven for wanting more evidence before accepting this premise.

Finally, Ackman concludes by suggesting that Hertz' fleet of 500,000 vehicles could make it a great partner for Uber (UBER). I grant the possibility that there could be some synergies from a potential partnership. But I wouldn't attach much valuation to this scenario until we got a much more concrete demonstration of a deal and the potential economics from it.

Why I'm Not Buying The Bullish Pitch

In his tweet, Ackman credited the management team with a "successful operational turnaround plan". I just don't see it, at least not in the numbers to date. Here are Hertz' Q4 2024 results:

The company saw worsening stats for most metrics. Q4's silver lining came from slightly higher fleet utilization, but that can be explained by the reduction in fleet size.

Ackman credits the team for successful handling of the fleet adjustment program, for example, yet we see in the commentary that the company is taking higher-than-expected losses on sale. At another point in the commentary, higher insurance and rent expense headwinds are blamed for "masking productivity" at the business.

It's possible that Hertz will become a successful turnaround in the future. That said, the Q4 results don't look like the sort of thing that you typically see when a company has already come out of a prior slump. And, let's not forget, the company was still operating in a fairly strong economic environment in Q4 of 2024.

It's perfectly reasonable to wonder if things such as fleet utilization will go back down in the back half of 2025 as consumers pull back on spending. In particular, we've seen a sizable decline in international tourists arriving to the United States in recent weeks along with airlines such as United Airlines (UAL) offering more cautious guidance for the remainder of the year given the mounting macroeconomic uncertainty.

I'd also suggest there are deeper structural issues at Hertz than just the unfortunate Tesla vehicle order which led to large operating losses and depreciation charges. Take the pandemic period, for example. Publicly-traded peer Avis Budget Group (CAR) made a windfall during that time as used vehicle prices soared and Avis, unlike Hertz, cashed in on the opportunity:

Notably, however, Avis stock has struggled over the past year. Investors had been hoping for a decline in interest rates, which would have made it easier for car rental companies to manage their debts. Also, lower interest rates tend to lead to firmer used car prices, all else held equal. CAR stock has slumped dramatically since the end of 2023 as investors are taking a less optimistic view of the industry's intermediate-term outlook. Even the recent tariff-related catalyst for used car prices hasn't made much of an impact for Avis shares.

This casts into doubt Ackman's contention that the tariffs are going to be a game-changer in terms of Hertz's valuation. Meanwhile, Avis is strongly profitable, has a more proven capital allocation track record, and is selling at less than 10x projected forward earnings even after this week's rally. For investors that are bullish on the car rental stock theme, Avis seems like a much safer choice than the highly-levered and unprofitable Hertz.

Hertz Stock Verdict

I want to qualify my sell rating here. Consider it more of an avoid. For investors that have owned Hertz shares for a while, this recent share price surge makes for an excellent opportunity to get out of that position at a much more attractive level. And for traders that happened to ride alongside Ackman with this move, that's turned out well and this looks like a great time to cash those chips in.

I personally would be shy about outright short selling Hertz shares here. There were already other large institutional holders in Hertz prior to Pershing Square getting involved. Knighthead Capital alone owns nearly 60% of Hertz. This means that the free float is quite small. For example, HTZ stock was not available for short sale for part of the day on Thursday at Interactive Brokers. When that brokerage can't find borrow, it's usually indicative of a highly-shorted stock that is prone to the possibility of sharp short-term price squeezes. With a well-known investor involved and a small free float of stock, Hertz shares could do just about anything in the near-term.

Over the longer-haul, call it the next six or twelve months, I would assign a high probability that Hertz trades back down to $5/share or less. Potentially much less if the company ends up going down a debt restructuring path by then. But the road to get there might be fairly windy, at least over the next month or two. While I am negative of Hertz long-term however, be cautious and prudent in terms of position sizing if playing the short side directly.

Hertz has a ton of debt and was losing large amounts of money even when the economy was strong. Heading into a potential recession, this could further pressure Hertz' already shaky balance sheet. Ackman's involvement may help Hertz refinance debt or issue additional equity to raise funds. Still, risk seems clearly to the downside from Hertz stock following the recent doubling of the share price.

data.title: RTX Q1 Preview: Don't Expect Fireworks (Just Yet)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tuesday, April 22nd, next week is going to be a big day for the Defense sector with three out of the Big 4 of US defense companies reporting their Q1 earnings for 2025.

RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) is one of those three companies slated to report its Q1 CY25 earnings results on Tuesday morning next week. While I expect the company to continue its streak of positive earnings beats in the Q1 report, I strongly believe markets will be closely watching the company’s backlog to look for any significant changes amidst the backdrop of tariffs.

On one hand, RTX’s defense business has been booming for the company, with strong sales visibility and a healthy book-to-bill ratio seen in its defense backlog. On the other hand, RTX’s commercial business is still being haunted by the ghosts of its “powder metal issue” that plagued its GTF (Geared Turbofan) engines.

The key question is if the company can grow its business given the above crosscurrents, especially with supply chains around the world possibly feeling pressure from the volatile nature of tariffs.

Per my analysis below, RTX remains a Hold as it heads into earnings next week.

RTX’s Commercial Business May Continue To Drag

Based on RTX’s full-year results for 2024, the company is now an $80B business, growing at a CAGR of 9.3% since 2020. Throughout these years, RTX’s P&W (Pratt & Whitney) business that designs, manufactures, and services aircraft engines has been a key catalyst for RTX, growing at a CAGR of 13.7%, outpacing the company’s overall consolidated growth pace.

Then came RTX’s infamous powder metal issue, which I suspect is still limiting the growth potential for RTX. Agreed that management navigated the company & its customers quite well through the situation, but the issue still persists in my view, limiting the additional scope of growth for the P&W GTF engine program. This is something I had observed in my previous earnings preview of RTX, where I noted that RTX could be range-bound for a while until this issue is fully cleared.

P&W GTF engines are regularly used in the narrow-body aircraft market, where the company holds a dominant market share. Some of RTX’s large customers, like Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY), have been very vocal about the issues they still face from the CY23 powder metal issues.

Two months ago, Airbus’ management singled out P&W in a call with investors in February when asked to comment on engine suppliers:

No specific comments on the widebodies, and we get from Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) what we need for 2025 and onwards.

There are challenges on engines for our narrow bodies. On the GTF of Pratt & Whitney, we are still limited by the aftermath of the metal powder issues two years ago and the trajectory that was redefined in two waves of negotiation with them.

Emphasis by author.

Airbus did note that P&W is on a positive trajectory, but the support being provided to Airbus has come at the cost of lesser support for new aircraft engines, which still grounds a large number of the Airbus fleet.

Airbus management’s comments on P&W came a day after RTX management struck a more positive tone in a Barclays conference about their progress on providing their customers support on the powder metal issue:

Look, I think that we're not going to wake up on January 1, 2027, and have the entire fleet cleared. But we anticipate being in a much, much more manageable position as a fleet and be able to support our customers and Airbus the way they would expect.

In my view, until RTX management can restore full confidence in its customers about completely putting this powder metal issue behind them, the Commercial business will be sluggish.

The sluggish growth can also be seen in RTX’s Commercial backlog of $125B in CY24, which reported its first full year of mid-single-digit growth last year versus the previous years of low-teens growth.

If the upcoming Q1 CY25 quarter can show RTX’s Commercial backlog picking up pace beyond the 5.9% pace seen last year, that would indicate some early signs about a bottom in the deceleration of the Commercial backlog.

Can Patriot's Contribution To The Defense Backlog Continue?

Contrary to the sluggish growth seen in RTX’s Commercial business, the Defense business is keeping RTX busy.

As seen in Exhibit B above, RTX’s Defense business saw the fastest increase in its backlog last year, growing 25.6% y/y to $9.8B. International strength was one of the main drivers of the backlog. In terms of products, RTX’s Patriot program as well as its F135 program drove many contract wins for RTX last year.

I think RTX has a very good chance of reporting another strong quarter of defense backlog growth based on the contract wins that RTX has already announced through Q1.

RTX started its 2025 with its Patriot product, continuing its strong performance of contract wins. RTX announced two separate Patriot contract wins in Q1—one was the ~$1B contract to supply additional Patriot systems to Romania, while the other was a half-a-billion-dollar contract win where RTX will replenish the Dutch Patriot systems.

Outside of the Patriot program, P&W won a $1.5B contract with the US Air Force in February this year to continue providing maintenance services for its F119 engine that powers the Air Force’s F-22 Raptor stealth fighter jets.

To me, it looks like RTX could see another strong quarter in its Defense backlog.

Above everything, I am very interested in understanding management’s view on the pre-solicitation notice of a massive $50B contract that the DLA (Defense Logistics Agency) may award to the company.

DLA is structuring this contract as a sole-source contract, meaning the winning vendor would provide production systems & support for the Patriot missile defense systems. If & when this contract is awarded, I believe it could be a game changer for RTX. However, I do not expect DLA’s contract to be announced any time soon. This is a mega contract that covers many defense agencies and would require significant planning and coordination on their part. Nevertheless, I will be keenly interested in how management characterizes their view on the DLA notice.

RTX Stock Valuation Premiums Look Tight In A Range

Heading into earnings, RTX’s valuation premium looks to be locked in a tight range with no room for expansion or contraction.

Comparing RTX to itself, I see that the company trades at a forward valuation of ~21x, which is ~9% from the ~23x it traded at last year. So if markets were to test RTX’s valuation premium expansion case, RTX’s stock price could reach $140, which is close to the average target price of $144.

But if optimism were to fall in RTX after its Q1 earnings results, I believe markets can re-evaluate RTX’s average valuation premium of ~19x. The contraction in premium implies a 9-10% drop in the stock price, bringing it down to the ~$116 area.

So I am expecting a +9 to -9% move in RTX’s stock post the Q1 earnings results unless there is a significant catalyst that management announces on the call.

What To Look For In RTX's Q1 Earnings Report

Here are some of the estimates investors should look out for in the Q1 report:

  • Q1 consensus estimates: EPS of $1.37 on revenues of $19.8B, growing 2.6% y/y.
  • Full-year CY25 consensus estimates: $6.11 EPS on revenues of $84.2B, growing 4.3% y/y. Both these estimates are above management’s midpoint projections of $6.8 EPS on revenues of $83.5B, growing 3.4% y/y.
  • Management commentary on backlog, international business, impacts of tariffs, and supply chain health.

On tariffs, management noted in the Barclays conference that the company is much better positioned this time around, with their supply chain more diversified worldwide. In that February conference, management believed they were “well positioned as we head in here in 2025,” with respect to tariffs.

Takeaway

RTX Corp. looks steady as it heads into the earnings report next week. Weakness in its Commercial business looks to be offset by strength in its Defense business. The performance of these two businesses will mostly continue in a similar way in Q1 as well, in my view, unless significant catalysts can change the outlook for RTX.

RTX continues to warrant a Hold rating in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
